ttps.evidence_snippet: Play ransomware actors obtain and abuse existing account credentials, likely purchased on the dark web, to gain initial access
Play ransomware actors exploit FortiOS (CVE-2018-13379, CVE-2020-12812), Microsoft Exchange ProxyNotShell (CVE-2022-41040, CVE-2022-41082), and SimpleHelp RMM (CVE-2024-57727) vulnerabilities
Play ransomware actors use external-facing services such as RDP and VPN for initial access
Play ransomware actors leveraged PowerShell commands to achieve RCE with CVE-2024-57727
Play ransomware actors use GRIXBA to enumerate network information and identify network configurations
Play ransomware actors use GRIXBA to scan for antivirus software

ttps.technique: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
ttps.evidence_snippet: Play ransomware actors use GMER, IOBit, and PowerTool to disable antivirus software; PowerShell scripts target Microsoft Defender

ttps.technique: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
ttps.evidence_snippet: Play ransomware actors remove log files and delete logs to hide intrusion activity
The Play ransomware binary is recompiled for every attack, resulting in unique hashes for each deployment, complicating detection
Play ransomware actors search for unsecured credentials stored insecurely on a compromised network
Play ransomware actors use Mimikatz credential dumper to gain domain administrator access
Play ransomware actors use WinPEAS to search for privilege escalation paths
Play ransomware actors distribute executables within the compromised environment

ttps.technique: Domain Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification
ttps.evidence_snippet: Play ransomware actors distribute executables via Group Policy Objects

ttps.technique: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility
ttps.evidence_snippet: Play ransomware actors split compromised data into segments and use WinRAR to compress files into .RAR format
Play ransomware actors use WinSCP to transfer data from a compromised network to actor-controlled accounts
Files are encrypted with AES-RSA hybrid encryption using intermittent encryption (every other 0x100000 bytes); .PLAY extension added
Play ransomware group uses a double extortion model, encrypting systems after exfiltrating data, for financial gain

summary: FBI, CISA, and ACSC advisory AA23-352A (updated June 4, 2025) on Play (Playcrypt) ransomware group, one of the most active ransomware groups in 2024. As of May 2025, approximately 900 entities affected globally across North America, South America, and Europe since June 2022. Initial access via valid accounts purchased on dark web (T1078), exploitation of FortiOS (CVE-2018-13379, CVE-2020-12812), Microsoft Exchange ProxyNotShell (CVE-2022-41040/41082), and SimpleHelp RMM (CVE-2024-57727 exploited January 2025), plus RDP/VPN external remote services. Custom tools include GRIXBA information-stealer for network enumeration and AV detection. Defense evasion via GMER, IOBit, PowerTool to disable AV; log deletion; per-attack binary recompilation (unique hashes per deployment). Lateral movement via Cobalt Strike, SystemBC, PsExec; credential dumping via Mimikatz; privilege escalation via WinPEAS. Exfiltration via WinRAR compression + WinSCP transfer. AES-RSA hybrid intermittent encryption (.PLAY extension), ransom notes (ReadMe.txt) direct victims to gmx.de/web.de emails; double-extortion model with TOR leak site. ESXi variant targets VMs with specific file extensions. IOCs include 16 file hashes (SHA256/SHA1), 2 YARA rules (PlayForESXi, GRXBA), Suricata rules for GRIXBA web history scanning detection.

timeline.event: Play ransomware group first observed impacting businesses and critical infrastructure
timeline.evidence_snippet: Since June 2022, the Play (also known as Playcrypt) ransomware group has impacted a wide range of businesses and critical infrastructure

timeline.event: First Play ransomware incident observed in Australia
timeline.evidence_snippet: In Australia, the first Play ransomware incident was observed in April 2023

timeline.event: Most recent Play ransomware incident observed in Australia (as of original December 2023 advisory publication)
timeline.evidence_snippet: In Australia, most recently in November 2023

timeline.event: Initial version of AA23-352A advisory published
Multiple ransomware groups including Play-tied initial access brokers exploit CVE-2024-57727 in SimpleHelp RMM tool following vulnerability disclosure
timeline.evidence_snippet: Multiple ransomware groups exploited CVE-2024-57727 in remote monitoring and management (RMM) tool SimpleHelp following the vulnerability's disclosure on 16 January 2025

timeline.event: FBI investigations of Play ransomware continue through January 2025 (most recent investigation timeframe cited in advisory)
timeline.evidence_snippet: FBI, CISA, and ACSC are releasing this joint advisory to disseminate Play ransomware group's IOCs and TTPs identified through FBI investigations as recently as January 2025

timeline.event: As of May 2025, FBI aware of approximately 900 affected entities allegedly exploited by Play ransomware actors
timeline.evidence_snippet: As of May 2025, FBI was aware of approximately 900 affected entities allegedly exploited by the ransomware actors

timeline.event: Updated version of AA23-352A advisory published with new TTPs, current IOCs, ESXi variant details, YARA/Suricata rules
timeline.evidence_snippet: Update June 4, 2025: The advisory was updated to reflect new TTPs employed by Play ransomware group, as well as provide current IOCs/remove outdated IOCs
